content:
@Benno Lossin I have an issue with pin_init that I cannot figure out. I have this code:

Ok(configfs::Group::new(
            name.try_into()?,
            item_type,
            // TODO: cannot coerce new_mutex!() to impl PinInit<_, Error>, so put mutex inside
            try_pin_init!( DeviceConfig {
                data <- new_mutex!( DeviceConfigInner {
                    powered: false,
                    disk: None,
                    name: name.try_into()?,
                }),
            }),
        ))

DeviceConfig implements PinInit. I would like to get rid of DeviceConfig and just use a Mutex<DeviceConfigInner>. But I cannot pass the result of new_mutex!(...) as a function argument of type impl PinInit<_, Error>, because new_mutex!(...) returns an impl PinInit<_, Infallible>. Is there some kind of trick to do a conversion here?
Code in context is here
you could try Ok(new_mutex!()?) though that is somewhat ugly
ah this is annoying...

Fiona Behrens said:

you could try Ok(new_mutex!()?) though that is somewhat ugly

that won't work, since new_mutex is of type impl PinInit<_, Infallible> and not of Result<_, Error>
I don't think that there is a conversion function, but it should be rather easy to implement...
bit confused by the code. does the new_mutex bubble up the error from name.try_into()?? or is that getting lost somewhere

Fiona Behrens said:

bit confused by the code. does the new_mutex bubble up the error from name.try_into()?? or is that getting lost somewhere

that gets bubbled up, but the error is delayed until the initializer is used
no wait, I was confused, that gets bubbled up immediately

nope wait, got confused again... the first name.try_into()? gets bubbled up directly inside of the function. The second name.try_into()? is inside of the try_pin_init! macro, which keeps the error until the initializer is run
so essentially name.try_into()? is delayed until the initializer is run

but to get back to the issue, I can add a conversion function, or see if it's possible to implement PinInit<T, E> for I where I: PinInit<T, Infallible> (though that's probably not possible due to orphan rules)
alternatively, we could have a try_new_mutex! macro that returns an impl PinInit<Mutex<T>, E>

Benno Lossin said:

but to get back to the issue, I can add a conversion function, or see if it's possible to implement PinInit<T, E> for I where I: PinInit<T, Infallible> (though that's probably not possible due to orphan rules)

This seems like the best solution to me. Isn't there some option that would allow us to bypass the orphan rule?

Benno Lossin said:

alternatively, we could have a try_new_mutex! macro that returns an impl PinInit<Mutex<T>, E>

This would cascade to any type that has a new_.*! initializer. Not sure how many that is.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

but to get back to the issue, I can add a conversion function, or see if it's possible to implement PinInit<T, E> for I where I: PinInit<T, Infallible> (though that's probably not possible due to orphan rules)

This seems like the best solution to me. Isn't there some option that would allow us to bypass the orphan rule?

I actually don't think it's the orphan rule that's biting us here. The impl just overlaps, since E can be instantiated as Infallible...

Benno Lossin said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

but to get back to the issue, I can add a conversion function, or see if it's possible to implement PinInit<T, E> for I where I: PinInit<T, Infallible> (though that's probably not possible due to orphan rules)

This seems like the best solution to me. Isn't there some option that would allow us to bypass the orphan rule?

I actually don't think it's the orphan rule that's biting us here. The impl just overlaps, since E can be instantiated as Infallible...

Could it be done for PinInit<T, Error> ? Not as generic, but better than nothing.
hmm I'll check, that might be possible
There should already be a From impl for Error form Infallible, right?
yes, but since Infallible is uninhabited, you create any value from it
so you don't need the From impl

Andreas Hindborg said:

Could it be done for PinInit<T, Error> ? Not as generic, but better than nothing.

that also overlaps with another impl...
